lesson: Sloped sump concrete (e.g. a base sloped at 5%) is a separate additive volume, not a deduction. Compute it as a tapered wedge: V = 0.5 × (h_start + h_end) × width × length ÷ 1728 ÷ 27, where h_end = h_start + slope_rate × length_in. For example, a 5% slope over a 480” × 545” base starting at 11” yields ~138 CY that must be listed as its own positive line item.

ai_example.name: Sumps (deduction from base slab)
ai_example.notes: Negative volume: two sump depressions cast into base slab.

user_example.correction_note: There is a section of concrete on sheet S4 in addition to the base slab. Sump concrete starts at 11" height and increases at 5%. Volume=0.5*(11"+38.25")*480"*545"/46656=138.07 yd³. Must be included as a positive additive element.

lesson: When a roof slab drawing shows different section thicknesses or multiple annotated depths, extract each section as a separate element with its correct depth rather than applying one uniform thickness across the entire footprint.

ai_example.name: Roof Slab
ai_example.notes: Same exterior footprint, thickness 1'-2" per Section B/S4.

user_example.correction_note: There are two roof slabs of different depths — each section must be calculated separately with its own annotated thickness.

lesson: Interior dividing walls are typically thinner than perimeter walls; always find an explicit dimension callout for interior walls rather than defaulting to the perimeter wall thickness. If the section or plan does not label the interior wall, flag it as uncertain and note the assumption clearly.

user_example.correction_note: The center dividing wall has a thickness of 1'-0", not 1'-8" like the perimeter walls.
